title: Trade credit and the stability of supply chains
abstract: We show that trade credit flows increase when a firm in a production network becomes a less reliable supplier due to an operating shock. Affected firms extend more trade credit when their customers have lower switching costs or expect more disruption. Suppliers that are more dependent on the affected firms facilitate the trade credit extension. However, when financial constraints at the affected firms and their suppliers prevent the increase in trade credit, customers sever their relationships with the affected firms, and the sales of the affected firms and their suppliers drop, suggesting that trade credit enhances production network stability.

author.Nuri Ersahin: Cox School of Business at Southern Methodist University, United States
author.Mariassunta Giannetti*: Stockholm School of Economics, the Swedish House of Finance, CEPR, and ECGI, Sweden
author.Ruidi Huang: Cox School of Business at Southern Methodist University, United States

article.1. Introduction: Most studies of trade credit focus on bilateral supplier-customer relationships, thus considering firms as either a borrower or a lender. However, in the real world, firms are part of complex production networks and simultaneously act as borrowers and lenders. Technological complementarities, incentives, and hold up problems along the supply chain are crucial for the success of upstream and downstream companies. Financial claims and obligations interlocking firms along the supply chain could be used to improve incentives and enhance supply chain stability, as suggested by theoretical work by Kim and Shin (2012). However, we still have scant knowledge of the mechanisms through which trade credit lubricates supply chain operations and on whether it indeed enhances their stability.This paper tests whether trade credit flows indeed serve as a “glue” to cement supply chains by exploring changes in trade credit usage around events that imperil the supply chain's survival. We also investigate the extent to which changes in trade credit provision depend on the incentives and constraints faced by the supply chain participants, and crucially, the effects of trade credit on the stability of customer-supplier relationships.We conjecture that operating shocks impair firms’ reliability as suppliers of goods and services and explore for the first time how interdependencies over the supply chain affect the provision of trade credit in their aftermath.2 Operating shocks affect a firm's ability to deliver timely and high-quality goods and services to customers and may arise from natural disasters, strikes, cyberattacks, geopolitical tensions, wars, or simply accidents. In most of our analysis, we consider temporary disruption to operations caused by natural disasters. Natural disasters are known to disrupt firms’ operations and to propagate upstream and downstream (see, e.g., Acemoglu et al., 2012; Barrot and Sauvagnat, 2016; Carvalho et al., 2021) and have the advantage of being plausibly orthogonal to other firms’ shocks. However, we argue and provide some evidence that our results are more general and, for instance, hold when operating difficulties arise from uncertainty about a firm's ability to source inputs.We build a simple framework in which operating shocks decreasing a firm's reliability as a supplier in expectation have the largest negative effects on the production of downstream firms, which may incur losses if inputs are delivered late or are of inferior quality. The customers of affected firms may thus have incentives to look for other suppliers to substitute or complement those experiencing operational difficulties. Since customers are difficult to replace, the interruption of existing relationships would be costly for the affected firms. Thus, in an attempt to preserve the relationship as the expected quality of their services weakens, firms experiencing operational difficulties may transfer surplus to their customers through trade credit. This would be consistent with theories and empirical evidence that firms use trade credit to transfer surplus to their customers and expand their customer base (Klapper et al., 2012; Murfin and Njoroge, 2015; Barrot, 2016; Breza and Liberman, 2017; Giannetti et al., 2021; Grigoris et al., 2023).The effects however are unlikely to stop at the affected firm and its customers. As Glode and Opp (2023) note, if firms are interconnected through sequential trade credit claims, their ability to extend liquidity to their customers depends on their own liabilities and on whether they expect those to be renegotiated. More generally, given that a firm's revenue depends on the revenues of its customers (Elliott et al., 2014), lower sales for an affected firm with shrinking customer base would also translate into smaller sales for their suppliers. Put differently, the specific production network to which a firm belongs is important for its revenue. The suppliers of affected firms have a stake in the stability of the whole supply chain and want to avoid the breakup of the relationships between the affected firms and their customers. Thus, the suppliers may extend more trade credit in order to facilitate the extension of the affected firms’ trade credit.Using customer-supplier linked data, we show that firms that are affected by natural disasters provide more trade credit to their customers and receive more trade credit from their suppliers. We are able to confirm this finding using hand-collected trade credit data that allow us to observe trade credit flows between customers and suppliers and to identify the supply and demand of trade credit.The finding that the accounts payable and the accounts receivable of firms facing operational difficulties simultaneously increase would be hard to reconcile without considering supply chain operations, as we do in our framework. If trade credit were to flow from firms with easy access to finance to firms experiencing negative shocks, as theories based on the suppliers’ financial advantage would suggest (Petersen and Rajan, 1997), we should observe that the accounts payable of affected firms increase, while their accounts receivable do not vary or even decrease. However, affected firms simultaneously provide and use more trade credit, indicating that firms obtain trade credit from their suppliers and pass it on to their customers, even if this behavior drains their liquidity and may have negative consequences on investment (Murfin and Njoroge, 2015).We perform a number of cross-sectional tests to shed light on the mechanisms driving our findings. First, we analyze how switching costs affect the provision of trade credit. Customers purchasing products that are easy to substitute face lower switching costs (Cunat, 2007). Hence, firms supplying these products may have to transfer more surplus to maintain their customers when they face operational difficulties. We show that affected firms that produce easy to substitute products indeed extend more trade credit to their customers. In addition, suppliers extend more trade credit to the affected firms if the customers of the latter can easily switch to alternative suppliers of the input. The fact that not only the affected firms, but also their suppliers provide more trade credit indicates that suppliers accommodate the provision of liquidity, which is essential for the survival of the supply chain.Second, we consider that the extent to which customers expect the operations of a firm to be disrupted by natural disasters should matter for trade credit flows. Accordingly, we observe that firms that can be expected to deliver high-quality and timely inputs and services after a natural disaster, because they have operations in many locations or have matured a strong reputation with the customers, extend and receive less trade credit.Third, we show that the propensity of firms affected by natural disasters and their suppliers to extend trade credit depends on their stakes in the survival of the production network. Trade credit flows increase more if the affected firm is highly dependent on a major customer because the severance of the relationship would imply a larger drop in sales for the affected firm and its suppliers. Furthermore, suppliers provide more trade credit if they are highly dependent on the affected firm. Overall, these findings suggest that firms use trade credit to enhance supply chains’ survival.To shed additional light on the mechanisms driving our findings, we investigate the effects of financial constraints that limit trade credit provision on the stability of supply chains. We find that following natural disasters, affected firms’ accounts payable do not increase if their suppliers are financially constrained. Affected firms do not extend the payment terms to their customers if both they and their suppliers are financially constrained. Customers that do not receive more trade credit in turn become more likely to terminate their relationships with the affected firms and start new relationships. Consequently, the affected firms’ and their suppliers’ sales decrease. Thus, pervasive financial constraints over the supply chain limit trade credit usage and imperil the stability of supply chains. On the contrary, supply chains remain stable when the affected firms are financially constrained, but their suppliers are not. In this case, thanks to the liquidity provided by their suppliers, the affected firms are able to extend more trade credit, even if they are financially constrained, and the consequences on the sales of the affected firm and its suppliers are limited.This paper contributes to several strands of the literature. First, by exploring trade credit usage in response to operating shocks, we are the first to show empirically that trade credit is used as a glue to enhance the stability of production networks. The literature has so far focused on bilateral relationships between customers and suppliers and emphasized the role of financial constraints and customer bargaining power (see, e.g., Petersen and Rajan, 1997; Cunat, 2007; Giannetti et al., 2011; Klapper et al., 2012). A notable exception is Gofman and Wu (2022), who document a number of stylized facts regarding a firm's position in the production network and trade credit provision. Our finding can help explain why Gofman and Wu (2022) find that central and upstream firms provide more trade credit: These firms are indirectly affected by more downstream shocks through their customers and extend trade credit to enhance the stability of their supply chains. We show that trade credit provision depends not only on the characteristics of a firm's customers, but also on the characteristics of the customers’ customers. In addition, while previous literature emphasizes that trade credit can emerge because a firm has strong relationships with its clients (Wilner, 2000; McMillan and Woodruff, 1999), we show that it also enhances the stability of direct and indirect customer-supplier links.Second, a growing body of research documents how the transmission of shocks over production networks affects the performance of customers and suppliers and ultimately leads to shock propagation and aggregate fluctuations (see, e.g., Hertzel et al., 2008; Barrot and Sauvagnat, 2016; Giroud and Mueller, 2019; Cen et al., 2020; Carvalho et al., 2021; Pankratz and Schiller, 2021). In this context, trade credit has been shown to help explain the propagation of negative shocks as firms default on their suppliers (Kyiotaki and Moore, 1997; Boissay and Gropp, 2013; Jacobson and van Schedvin, 2015). Several papers document that firms that face liquidity shocks obtain more trade credit from their suppliers and extend less trade credit to their customers (see, e.g., Love et al., 2007; Garcia-Appendini and Montoriol-Garriga, 2013; Restrepo et al., 2019; Costello, 2020; Alfaro et al., 2021; Amberg et al., 2021). Since firms affected by liquidity shocks provide less liquidity to their customers, trade credit is considered to amplify the initial shock.The literature is silent on the effects of operating shocks on trade credit provision. We introduce to the literature on trade credit the distinction between liquidity and operating shocks and argue that operating shocks are more pernicious than liquidity shocks for supply chain stability because firms’ ability to produce is temporarily impaired and affected firms may cause losses for their customers (Acharya et al., 2022). Liquidity shocks do not hamper a firm's ability to produce and therefore do not affect as much as operating shocks the value of the relationships for the affected firms’ customers. Following a liquidity shock, customers have weak incentives to search for more reliable suppliers of the products and are willing to renounce some trade credit to save on switching costs and avoid a supplier's default on its financiers. Accordingly, following liquidity shocks, we do not expect an increase in credit flows to cement supply chain relationships. We provide a conceptual framework to explore how the financial structure of the supply chain adapts to operating shocks. Our results imply that trade credit enhances the stability of production networks after operating shocks.
article.2. Conceptual framework and testable imp: We consider a three-step production process, in which the most upstream firm, Firm 2, delivers an input to Firm 1, which transforms it and delivers it to Firm 0. The output at each of the three steps of production is I, aI, and a2I, where a>1. For simplicity, we assume that there is a homogeneous good or, equivalently, that outputs are expressed in a numeraire. Therefore, net of the payment for any inputs, the profits of each stage of production are: I, I(a-1), and aI(a-1).3In the empirical analysis, we consider the case in which an operating shock affects Firm 1. Specifically, we consider an operating shock that occurs at t=0, before I is shipped by Firm 2 and paid. Following such an operating shock, the expected output of Firm 0 to be realized at t=1 incorporates the probability that the input is delivered late or is of inferior quality, disrupting Firm 0′s production process. The expected output of Firm 0 after an operating shock affecting Firm 1 is: x a2I, where 0<1-x<1 is the probability of disruption, arising from the fact that Firm 1 may deliver the input late or the input may be of inferior quality.Following an operating shock to Firm 1, the expected profits of Firm 0 are consequently aI(xa-1). Firm 0 can avoid disruption by switching to another supplier at t=0. Changing supplier implies switching costs K. Thus, Firm 0 switches when Firm 1 has experienced an operating shock if aI(xa-1)<aI(a-1)-K. That is, if K<(1-x) a2I.If Firm 0 switches, we assume that it is hard for Firm 2 and Firm 1 to find new customers to replace Firm 0. Consequently, Firm 1′s and Firm 2′s profits are equal to zero if Firm 0 switches supplier and the supply chain collapses. If the operating shock occurs, but Firm 0 does not switch supplier, for simplicity, we assume that the sales of Firm 1 and Firm 2 are as large as without the operating shock. Thus, the total surplus that the supply chain loses if Firm 0 switches is: aI+K.Note that not only the affected firm would lose I(a-1), but also its supplier has a stake in the survival of the supply chain because it would lose, I. These assumptions capture that operating shocks have negligible negative effects if the supply chain remains stable. Yet, they may have considerable negative effects on the affected firm and its suppliers if the production network is disrupted. For these reasons, in the absence of financial constraints, both upstream firms have incentives to share part of their future profits with Firm 0 to avoid the breakage of the supply chain. In this respect, Firm 2 internalizes the need to preserve downstream customer relationships for the survival of the supply chain. Specifically, if K<(1-x)a2I, Firm 1 and Firm 2 can prevent that Firm 0 switches to another supplier by transferring surplus.Following Giannetti et al. (2021), we assume that surplus is transferred through trade credit. This may be the case for the following two reasons. First, in the presence of multiple customers, as in Giannetti et al. (2021), trade credit allows firms to transfer different amounts of surplus to customers with different switching costs, without charging different input prices, a conduct that could be sanctioned by antitrust authorities. Second, trade credit can provide a guarantee for the punctuality and quality of the input delivery (Lee and Stowe, 1993; Long et al., 1993; Klapper et al., 2012). In this context, trade credit can emerge without any need to renegotiate if customers delay payments when uncertainty on the quality and timely delivery of goods and services increases, while affected firms do not enforce the repayment and continue to deliver the inputs to maintain their customer relationships.The subsidy through trade credit that Firm 1 provides to Firm 0 can be written as ϕ0aI. That is, Firm 1 allows Firm 0 to delay the payment for part or all the input purchase aI, providing credit at a cost below Firm 0′s cost of capital. The parameter ϕ0 capture the size of the subsidy that may depend both on the amount of credit provided and the extent to which this credit is subsidized. If trade credit is provided at zero cost, as an increasing number of studies surveyed in Giannetti (2023) shows, ϕ0 can be interpreted as the proportion of the purchase that Firm 0 can pay late. Trade credit thus alters Firm 0′s condition for switching supplier. The supply chain remains stable if: aI(xa+ϕ0-1)≥aI(a-1)-K.This yields the first set of empirical implications of our simple framework.Implication 1Firms affected by operating shocks extend more trade credit ϕ0aI if their customers have low switching costs K or the expected disruption due to the operating shock (1-x) is particularly high.In the empirical analysis, not only do we test that on average firms affected by operating shocks should extend more trade credit to their customers, but we also explore the cross-sectional implications considering plausible proxies for K and x. For instance, the switching costs of the customer of Firm 1 may depend on the availability of products that are close substitutes provided by other suppliers, but also on the characteristics of specific customers. Switching costs are largely fixed in nature as in our simple model and are particularly low for large customers (Draganska et al., 2010), which may thus require a larger transfer not to switch supplier. In this case, besides transferring surplus, as in Giannetti et al. (2021), trade credit may have the additional benefit of not affecting the cost of the marginal unit purchased by Firm 0, mitigating any costs from the cannibalization of Firm 1′s sales to other smaller customers with potentially higher switching costs (which are outside of our model). In sum, we expect that large firms with suppliers affected by operating shocks should receive more trade credit.Following an operating shock to its supplier, a customer's inclination to sever the relationship depends not only on its switching costs, but also on the expected disruption. Any characteristics of the affected firm that lower the expected disruption should be associated with less trade credit provision. Expected disruption should be lower for firms that operate in many locations, which can presumably continue to produce and ship the input from unaffected plants. Similarly, firms with more inventories may have finished products ready to ship to their customers, thus limiting backlogs and disruption for downstream firms.We also consider that the expected disruption may be lower for firms that have developed a strong reputation with their customers. Strong reputation is likely to be associated with relationship length, which may also reveal high switching costs for the customers that have tailored their production processes to those suppliers for a long time. Both effects would go in the direction of decreasing the provision of trade credit by the company experiencing operating shocks. Another proxy for reputation is linked to the superstar status of a company. The largest firms in an industry are often considered superstars (Autor et al., 2020; Gutierrez and Philippon, 2019). These firms are also harder to substitute for more reliable suppliers. Thus, we expect that they provide less trade credit when they are hit by operating shocks.The model also highlights that the output disruption occurring when a customer switches affects not only the direct supplier but also the upstream firms, that is, the supplier of the supplier. Both Firm 1′s and Firm 2′s propensity to provide trade credit depends on their stakes in the survival of the supply chain. Specifically, Firm 2′s stake consists of its profits from the sales to the affected firm. Importantly, on average, the amount of trade credit that Firm 2 needs to provide to guarantee the survival of the supply chain depends on Firm 0′s switching costs and expected disruption and not only on the characteristics of Firm 1.Also, our simple framework assumes that both Firm 1 and Firm 2 have only one customer. More realistically, firms have many customers. In the presence of holdup concerns related to relationship-specific investments, the suppliers’ loss from losing a relationship increases more than proportionally when a firm loses a major customer, a feature that is often considered to depend on the size of the buyer relative to the seller (Klein et al., 1978; Williamson, 1979; Chipty and Snyder, 1999; Inderst and Wey, 2007), because large customers are particularly difficult to replace. For these reasons, affected firms and their suppliers are expected to be particularly inclined to extend trade credit to major customers. Based on the above considerations, not only do we expect that affected firms extend more trade credit if they are particularly dependent on a large customer, but also that the suppliers of affected firms do so if they are highly dependent on the affected firm, as in this case the supply chain drives a larger share of Firm 2′s profits and Firm 2 thus has a larger stake in its survival. In addition, Firm 2 is expected to provide more trade credit if Firm 1 has a major customer that quitting would jeopardize both Firm 1′s and Firm 2′s sales. Implication 2 summarizes the above discussion.Implication 2Upstream firms’ propensity to extend trade credit after an operating shock depends on their stake in the supply chain's survival and the characteristics of Firm 0, affecting Firm 1′s liquidity needs.Firms’ financing costs also play an important role. While ϕ0aI is the value of the subsidy to Firm 0, the cost of this transfer for Firm 1 depends on its cost of capital and it is equal to ϕ1aI,where ϕ1>ϕ0 if Firm 1 is more financially constrained. Tighter financing constraints for Firm 1 imply higher financing costs. Thus, extending trade credit reduces the profits of Firm 1 to aI(1-ϕ1)-I. If Firm 1 is financially constrained and has a high cost of capital, its payoff from extending enough trade credit to satisfy Firm 0′s participation constraint may become negative. Thus, if ϕ1>1–1/a, the firm has no incentive to provide a transfer that satisfies Firm 0′s participation constraint. In this case, the provision of trade credit needs to be facilitated by the supplier, which can extend cheap liquidity to the affected firm in order for the supply chain to survive. Firm 2 will have incentives to do so as long as it is financially unconstrained and its profits cover the financing cost ϕ2: I(1-ϕ2)>0.Implication 3If the affected firm is financially constrained, the ability of Firm 2 to extend trade credit is crucial for the supply chain survival.Implications 2 and 3 highlight the role of the suppliers of affected firms. When both ϕ1 and ϕ2 are large, implying that the affected firm and its supplier are financially constrained, trade credit flows do not increase and the supply chain collapses.The provision of liquidity by suppliers to firms affected by negative shocks mirrors the findings of papers exploring how supply chains adapt when liquidity shocks occur (see, e.g., Costello, 2020; Amberg et al., 2021) because suppliers’ profits are jeopardized by the breakage of downstream customer-supplier relationships, regardless of the nature of the shock. Operating shocks differ from liquidity shocks for their effects on the customers, which are directly exposed to issues with the quality and timely delivery of the affected firm's products only when operating shocks occur. In the case of a liquidity shock, x is equal to 1 if Firm 1 has enough liquidity not to be forced to default by external financiers. Firm 0 is thus willing to renounce some trade credit to guarantee Firm 1′s survival and save the switching cost K.In what follows, we explore how trade credit flows vary following operating shocks and the effects of constraints to trade credit provision on the stability of supply chains.
article.3. Data and summary statistics: To test our hypotheses, we need information on supply chains, operating shocks, and financial information to measure trade credit flows and evaluate the cross-sectional implications of our theoretical framework. Below, we introduce our data and most important proxies.3.1. Supply chains and trade creditWe obtain firms’ financial information and the location of the headquarters from Compustat North America Fundamentals Quarterly Database. We exclude firms in the utility industries (SIC code 4900 – 4999), the financial services industries (SIC code 6000 – 6999), and government entities (SIC code 9000 – 9999) and construct two supply chain datasets, which complement each other.The first dataset merges Compustat with supply chain relationships from Factset Revere Supply Chain Relationship database. Factset Revere identifies customer and supplier relationships from SEC 10-K annual filings, investor presentations and press releases. Following Adelino et al. (2023), we make use of the reported information on customer and supplier relationships to create a comprehensive network of supply chain interconnections.4Factset Revere spans the period 2003 – 2019. Overall, we observe 7806 customers and 8306 suppliers. For the average firm in the sample, we observe 21 customers and 20 suppliers. Since Factset does not provide information on how much trade credit is used in a relationship, we proxy for trade credit flows using information on accounts payable and accounts receivable from Compustat. As is common in the literature (Petersen and Rajan, 1997), we scale accounts payable by the costs of goods sold and accounts receivable by sales to measure the amount of funding a firm receives from its suppliers and the provision of trade credit to its customers.5The second dataset relies on hand-collection of information on the amount of trade credit extended by a firm to its important customers as recorded in the 10-K disclosures to the SEC. Starting from 1990, the Financial Accounting Standard Board's (FASB) regulation No. 105 requires firms to disclose any concentration of credit risk.6 Typically, large amounts of accounts receivable to a major customer qualify as concentration of credit risk. In fact, page 12 of FASB 105 states: “A contractual right to receive cash in the future is a financial instrument. Trade accounts, notes, loans, and bonds receivable all have that characteristic.” Due to these requirements, firms may disclose the name of a customer and its accounts receivable balance, either as a dollar amount or as a percentage of total accounts receivable, in their annual reports (10-Ks). This source of disclosure is preferable for our purposes to information on major customers available through SFAS No. 14 (before 1997) and SFAS No. 131 (after 1997) because firms report the amount of receivables from a given customer.To collect these data, we use a procedure similar to that of Murfin and Njoroge (2015) and Freeman (2020). Specifically, we first download Compustat Customer Segment files from 1991 to 2019.7 From the Compustat Segment files, we observe major customers, defined as customers who account for at least 10% of the firm's sales, and the corresponding sales amount. For each of these firms, we extract all available 10-K filings from EDGAR. We then read all 10-K filings and look for information regarding major customers and concentration of credit risk. In the Internet Appendix (IA), Figure IA.1 presents excerpts from 10-Ks of firms reporting accounts receivable balances from specific customers. We manually collect the name of the customer, the sales amount (or as a percentage of total sales) to the customer, and the accounts receivable balance (or as a percentage of total accounts receivable) from the customer. Finally, we hand-match the customers to Compustat annual files by name. We require sales information between each customer and supplier pair in the Compustat Segment data to be non-missing to construct our variables of interest.We refer to this second dataset as the “SEC sample.” The final SEC sample has an annual frequency and includes 729 firms (both customers and suppliers) from 1991 to 2019. We observe a total of 317 customers and 430 suppliers. On average, we observe 1.59 customers and 2.15 suppliers per firm. While the SEC sample has limitations due to the small size and selection in firms’ reporting, its advantage is that we observe the amount of trade credit to a given customer. As we explain below, this will allow us to absorb non-parametrically customer (or supplier) unobserved heterogeneity and sharpen the interpretation of our main findings.3.2. Natural disastersOur conceptual framework implies that any shocks leading to operating difficulties, such as strikes, labor or input shortages, and regulations, should affect trade credit flows in the production network. We consider operating difficulties arising from natural disasters in a firm's headquarters county, because natural disasters are exogenous and unlikely to be correlated with firm shocks. In addition, they have been documented to lead to operating difficulties for the affected firms and their customers (Barrot and Sauvagnat, 2016), as implied by our theoretical framework.We identify the date and estimated damages of natural disasters as well as the FIPS codes of the affected counties using SHELDUS (Spatial Hazard and Loss Database for the United States), a database by the Center for Emergency Management and Homeland Security at Arizona State University.8 Following Barrot and Sauvagnat (2016), we consider natural disasters with estimated damages larger than 1 billion 2012 dollars. We require the disaster to last less than 30 days.The sample covers 42 disasters, including blizzards, earthquakes, floods, and hurricanes. These disasters affect a broad range of U.S. states and counties over the sample period. However, they are generally very localized and affect on average 47 counties, and at most 156 counties in a quarter, which is less than 5% of the total number of counties in the U.S.We introduce additional proxies in the empirical analysis and define all the variables in Appendix A. article.4. Main results: 4.1. Affected firmsThe main insight of our simple theoretical framework is that the provision of trade credit of a firm to its customers should increase if the firm experiences operating difficulties, which we proxy using natural disasters in the county of the headquarters. Using the Factset Revere sample, we estimate a difference-in-differences equation at the firm-quarter level similar to the one used by Barrot and Sauvagnat (2016):(1)ΔYi,t,t+4=β0+β1Affectedit+β2×Xi,t+ηi+ηt+ηj(i),y(t)+ηg(i),y(t)+εi,t,where Yi,t is typically either the ratio of accounts payable to the cost of goods sold or the ratio of accounts receivable to sales. We study how proxies for the use of trade credit and other firms’ policies change in the following four quarters. More specifically, we construct ΔYi,t,t+4 as the change in the outcome variable between quarters t + 4 and t. Our variable of interest, Affectedit, takes value equal to one if firm i is affected by a natural disaster in quarter t.9Throughout the analysis, we control for firm size, leverage, age, and profits as well as the exposure of the firm's customers and suppliers to the natural disaster. We also absorb unobserved heterogeneity by including firm (i) fixed effects, year-quarter (t) fixed effects, interactions of firm i’s industry (j(i)) and year (y(t)) fixed effects as well as interactions of the firm i’s state (g(i)) and year (y(t)) fixed effects. The inclusion of these fixed effects allows us to explore whether the behavior of firms in counties affected by natural disasters changes in the four quarters following the disaster in comparison to other firms in the same year, state, and industry. We cluster standard errors at the firm level, which is particularly important because our dataset includes overlapping quarters.Table 2 shows that in the year following a natural disaster, the ratio of accounts payable relative to the cost of goods sold increases by 8 percentage points (column 1). Contextually, affected firms’ receivables relative to sales increase by 2 percentage points indicating that they extend more trade credit to their customers (column 2).10 In fact, firms appear to entirely pass the extra liquidity received from their suppliers to their customers, as shown by the fact that in column 3 the net receivables, defined as accounts receivable minus accounts payable scaled by sales, are neither statistically nor economically different from zero.Table 2. The increase in affected firms’ payables however may not be an active decision of the suppliers. Affected firms may delay payments and suppliers may withhold or decrease new shipments. We find a positive and statistically significant coefficient on the dummy variable Customer of affected firm. Our conceptual framework suggests the mechanisms for why this may be the case. In the next section, we test these mechanisms by exploiting cross-sectional differences between firms and their customers and suppliers.

These firms and their suppliers would therefore have to extend less trade credit for the supply chain to remain stable after negative operating shocks occur. Columns 3 and 4 of Table 7 show that in accordance with this prediction of our conceptual framework, firms with a ratio of inventory to total assets above the median experience a 4% and 20.59% smaller increase in receivables and payables, respectively, following natural disasters.In addition, the expected disruption may also be lower when firms have developed a strong reputation with their customers. A strong reputation is likely to be associated with relationship length, which may also reveal high switching costs for customers that have tailored their production processes to those suppliers for a long time.15 Both effects would go in the direction of decreasing the need to transfer surplus to the customer and the provision of the trade credit to the company experiencing operating shocks. The firms with the highest market capitalization in an industry are often considered superstars (Autor et al., 2020; Gutierrez and Philippon, 2019). As we would expect, financially unconstrained firms, being able to facilitate trade credit flows, do not experience a drop in sales when their customers experience natural disasters even if their customers are financially constrained.Taken together, not only are these results consistent with the idea that trade credit enhances the stability of production networks when operating shocks affect a firm in the network but also suggest that firms affected by negative shocks can preserve their profitability thanks to trade credit flows.
article.7. Extensions: 7.1. Natural disasters and supplier choiceThe analysis so far has shown that suppliers with deep pockets can provide insurance. Since suppliers’ financial health can benefit a firm's performance, firms should pay close attention to the financial conditions of the suppliers (Titman, 1984; Banerjee et al., 2008). Specifically, we expect that firms that have the most to lose from negative realizations of operating risk seek the reliability provided by suppliers with financial flexibility. Table 14 provides some evidence that this is the case. For each firm, we consider the average size of the suppliers in a given year and industry. Given that larger suppliers are more likely to be financially unconstrained, we expect that firms that are more concerned about the negative consequences of operating shocks would be more likely to work with those suppliers.Table 14. This result suggests that financially constrained firms appreciate the insurance that larger suppliers can offer to a greater extent.7.2. Other operating shocks and comparison with liquidity shocksWe expect trade credit flows to increase when operating difficulties for a firm in the network imperil the survival of the supply chain. Natural disasters allow us to capture operating difficulties using an exogenous shock that is likely to be orthogonal to other firm shocks. But we expect the same effects to arise all the times in which disruptions due to strikes, labor shortages, cyberattacks, regulations, or difficulties in a firm's ability to obtain inputs impair the firm's reliability as a supplier of high quality and timely products and services.While it is hard to capture all the above events in a systematic way, we rely on an insight from Acharya et al. (2022), who argue that operating shocks arise from exposure to supply chain risk. We use the proxy for supply chain risk based on textual analysis of earnings conference calls of Ersahin et al. (2024). In Table 15, we show that firms that experience heightened supply chain risk extend more trade credit to their customers. These firms also receive more trade credit. While the accounts receivables of firms experiencing heightened supply chain risk may increase not only because their suppliers try to accommodate their liquidity needs but also because at least some of their suppliers have to compensate them for the expected disruption costs, these results support the external validity of our earlier findings.Table 15. In this case, customers have incentives to renounce some trade credit and accelerate payments to avoid incurring switching costs. We thus expect that firms experiencing liquidity shortfalls extend less trade credit to their customers, propagating downstream the liquidity shock. Table IA.13 shows that this is indeed the case. Firms that were associated with Lehman extend less trade credit to their customers after Lehman's failure, consistent with the results of previous literature on liquidity shocks (Costello, 2020). Overall, these results provide evidence for the external validity of our findings and further support the mechanisms behind our framework.
article.8. Conclusions: Supply chains are important for firms’ performance. But operating shocks due to natural disasters, cyberattacks, trade wars, and other sources threaten supply chain stability and survival. We show that supply chains remain stable when firms in the network are able to increase trade credit flows to increase the value of the relationship for their customers. The trade credit flows within the supply network following operating shocks suggest that not only affected firms but also their suppliers try to preserve the stability of the supply chain. Thus, suppliers extend trade credit to the affected firms, especially if the latter are financially constrained and would otherwise be unable to provide short-term funding to their customers.Production networks become unstable and the affected firms’ profitability decreases when financial constraints are pervasive and affect not only the affected firms, but also their suppliers. These problems may be far stronger for production networks that include small businesses. Our empirical analysis thus provides a lower bound for the negative effects of operating shocks on the instability of supply chains and provides support for policies, such as the American Rescue Plan, enacted at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic: Besides relieving a firm's financial constraints and supporting employment, these policies are likely to have sustained trade credit flows and the stability of the supply chains.
